title: Observation of an aperiodic polariton monotile
authors: Sergey Alyatkin, Yaroslav V. Kartashov, Kirill Sitnik, Philipp Grigoryev, Pavlos G. Lagoudakis
abstract: A plethora of unconventional localization phenomena and fractal features of linear spectrum observed in quasiperiodic structures have been accompanied by a long-standing quest for the geometrical elements and structures that permit tilings of the plane, but only in a non-periodic manner. Until 2024, it was believed that such quasiperiodic structures, or quasicrystals, could only be composed of at least two different tiles. Surprisingly, a newly discovered class of quasicrystals requires only one elementary monotile. However, its physical realization and study of propagating coherent excitations in this novel setting remained elusive. Here we optically sculpt aperiodic quasicrystals composed of "einstein" monotiles in an inorganic microcavity and observe nontrivial relative phases of the exciton-polariton condensates nonresonantly excited at the vertices of each monotile. Utilizing energy-resolved tomography in momentum-space, we reveal the formation of distinct Bragg peaks with six-fold symmetry and Dirac-like spectral fingerprints, intrinsic to the underlying graphene-like structure, while interferometric phase reconstruction shows a nontrivial synchronization pattern distinct from both periodic triangular lattices and Penrose quasicrystals. Our work demonstrates that monotiles can be converted into a programmable driven-dissipative artificial material, where long-range coherence coexists with enforced geometric aperiodicity, producing synchronization and spectral responses distinct from both periodic and conventional quasicrystalline tilings.
summary: This paper reports the first experimental realization of an aperiodic polariton lattice using the recently discovered 'einstein' monotile. By sculpting these tiles in a GaAs microcavity, the authors demonstrate that the unique geometry leads to distinct spectral features and a complex phase-locking pattern unlike any periodic or conventional quasicrystalline system.
main_problem: The study aims to realize and investigate the physical properties of a newly discovered class of aperiodic quasicrystals composed of a single 'einstein' (hat) monotile, specifically focusing on how this unique geometry affects the propagation of coherent excitations and phase synchronization.
main_result: The researchers successfully imprinted 'einstein' monotile structures in an inorganic microcavity, observing six-fold symmetric Bragg peaks, Dirac-like spectral features, and a complex phase-locking pattern that is distinct from both periodic and Penrose quasicrystalline tilings.
method: The study uses optical sculpting via a Spatial Light Modulator (SLM) to create the tiling, combined with energy-resolved momentum-space tomography and homodyne interferometry for phase reconstruction.
model_or_system: The system is an inorganic GaAs-based microcavity hosting nonresonantly excited exciton-polariton condensates, modeled by the 2D Gross-Pitaevskii equation coupled to a reservoir rate equation.
key_observables: Bragg peaks, Dirac-like spectral fingerprints, real-space polariton density, and relative phases of the condensates.
important_parameters: Lattice constant (D) ranging from 22.6 to 27.2 micrometers, number of tiles (M = 1, 4, 13), and pump power relative to threshold (P approx 1.15 P_thr).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the validity of the driven-dissipative Gross-Pitaevskii framework for describing the polariton dynamics in the microcavity.
figures_summary: Fig 1 compares different tiling geometries and shows real/reciprocal space PL for various tile numbers; Fig 2 presents 3D energy tomography and energy-resolved slices; Fig 3 compares phase locking patterns across periodic, Penrose, and 'einstein' lattices.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of the 'einstein' monotile, describes the experimental setup and optical sculpting technique, presents the spectral and spatial results of the aperiodic tiling, compares the phase synchronization of the monotile to other lattices, and concludes with numerical validation.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it demonstrates how a programmable, driven-dissipative artificial material can use aperiodicity to engineer complex, non-trivial synchronization and spectral responses.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Build-up of an aperiodic 2D tiling based on the “einstein” (or hat) monotile using microcavity polaritons. a Fragments of a periodic triangular lattice, Penrose quasicrystal, and an aperiodic tiling constructed with the “einstein” hat monotiles. b Experimental time-integrated real- space polariton photoluminescence (PL), excited by nonresonant structured pulsed laser pump in the shape of a single (M = 1) “einstein” monotile. c,d Show real-space polariton PL for the increased number of monotiles, M = 4 and M = 13, respectively. Overlaid dashed lines are to guide the eye and denote the position of the vertices with respect to the underlying lattice. e,f,g Corresponding measured...

Fig. 2 Energy tomography of 2D polariton quasicrystals based on the “einstein” tiling for M = 13. a Experimental time-integrated real-space polariton PL, excited by nonresonant pump. b,c,d Experimentally measured “slices” of energy paraboloid in reciprocal space [in (kx, ky) plane] at energies E = 1.97, 2.49, and 2.94 meV with respect to the bottom of the lower polariton branch. e Energy-resolved reciprocal space PL for kx = 0, schematically marked on c with vertical dashed line. The inset shows near perfectly linear dispersion. f Integrated total PL intensity in reciprocal space as a function of energy.

Fig. 3 Phase locking of polariton condensates in periodic and quasicrystalline struc- tures. a,b,c,d Experimental time-integrated real-space (top row) and reciprocal-space (bottom row) polariton PL above condensation threshold measured under CW excitation for the triangular lattice, the Penrose mosaic and the “einstein” monotiles for two different lattice constants of the underlying hexagonal lattice favouring either “in-phase” or “out-of-phase” synchronization between the nearest- neighbour condensates. Corresponding experimentally retrieved phase maps are shown in the middle row. Polaritons synchronize in-phase in periodic structure (a), yet adopt nontrivial phases in aperi- odic...
